This is a little bit more advanced, but just thought this would be worth sharing is understanding this concept of attribution. Meaning that if somebody makes a purchase, how do we know who should get credit for that purchase? So say, for example, you run Google Ads, and you run Facebook Ads. And somebody clicks on a Google Ad, and they don't buy. But then they come back, and a week later, they buy a Facebook ad. And they click a Facebook ad, and then they do buy. So who should get credit? So that's an age-old problem that there's no perfect solution for. So Google has this data-driven attribution model, which basically means that they're going to make decisions based on who they think, what traffic source really had the biggest influence. And they're going to give it credit, or sometimes they'll split it between multiple. But just know that you also have the options to say last click. And what that means is whatever they clicked last, if they click the Facebook Ad last, then Facebook gets 100% of the credit. Or first click, meaning if they clicked on the Google Ad first, then Google Ad gets 100% of the credit. But you've got all these various things that are really, really fancy. Bottom line is, most likely, don't worry about changing this. It's just really important if you're going to be somebody who specializes in statistics, is understanding that this is an age-old problem of attribution and knowing that when you're seeing stats in Facebook, you might be seeing stats that are also being credited by Google Ads because everybody wants to take credit for the sale.
